Brandon here and welcome back to the 10x business buying accelerator. So far I've talked about various types of due diligence you and your team need to perform before you close your deal. We covered financial, commercial, legal, cultural, operational, all the different aspects of doing the due diligence to understand everything you can about the business, its customers and its employees. In this lesson we're going to look at forecasting the business's cash flow before you close the deal. This forecasting is another part of your due diligence before the ink goes on the paper. And the reason we want to do this forecasting before you finalize the deal is because we want to make sure that the current business's cash flow is able to sustain the financing that you're leveraging. You might be able to commit and promise lenders this and that to get the money, but what's critical is that whatever you promise the business is able to actually perform and pay. You want to be sure you can be in a financially successful position after the purchase. And you want to make sure that nothing changes between the time that you started the deal until after closing it. The type of specific forecasting we're going to cover is called cash flow forecasting. The purpose of cash flow forecasting is to estimate the flow of cash coming in and out of the business over some period of time and in most cases day to day, month to month, quarter to quarter, year over year. In other words, you're attempting to model the business's financial liquidity over a specific time frame. And the reason we are using this type of forecasting is to essentially ignore revenue in place of real cash. Revenue isn't always a good indicator of how much cash a business will have at the beginning of a month. To issue $100,000 of invoices for work done in January that doesn't mean you'll get paid in January. It could be 30, 60 or 90 or 120 days. And that period of time, the business may not actually even have any cash. That money doesn't see the bank account until some future point in time. You have 100,000 on your balance sheet that you can't access. And if the invoice isn't paid on time, you might not even see the money for several months. So revenue can be misleading and create problems for you to try to expand and grow the business. When you're doing cash flow forecasting, you're ignoring sales on credit, your accounts payable and other accrued expenses. You're just looking at the cash that you expect to have month to month, adding in any cash you know that you'll have that month and subtracting any potential expenses you will pay out. For example, let's say it's mid August and you're trying to forecast cash flow for September. If you have $50,000 of operating cash on hand that you know you're not going to spend, you can assume you'll have that at the beginning of September. Next, you need to add in your sources of cash. That's any cash you'll receive in September. You're looking at receivables that will be collected or sales that will result in immediate cash. So let's say you wind up with $35,000 in cash sources. Next we need to deduct any uses of cash that will incur. These are such things as payroll, vendor payments, rent, loan payments, any fixed assets you plan to buy, etc. In other words, cash that you know will be leaving the business. Let's say you have $30,000 in cash leaving the business, so the actual cash change for the month becomes $5,000. Cash in minus cash out. That leaves you with an ending balance of $55,000, which then you'd carry over in October and the process repeats. Cash in, cash out. So you can see a brief look at how we'll calculate these projections. And later I'll share a template with you that you can use to do your own forecasting. But first, let's talk about the things you need to think about before you do the forecasting. It's important for you to understand where money in the business is flowing every month and determine how much cash flow you will need to cover the cost of financing as well as to fund a healthy business. Because if you get into the business and you're stuck trying to figure out how to pay for the financing, how to keep paying the employees, the other bills, the stuff you need to do every month, you're going to drive yourself mad, especially if you come up short. There are five key considerations before you begin your forecasting. Short term cash flow needs, interest and debt reduction needs, covenant and key date visibility needs, the business dependencies, the growth planning, generally speaking your day to day operating expenses. Let's go through each of these now. First we want to consider short term cash flow needs. So we're looking at daily, weekly or monthly payments needed for the business to operate. These are things like employee salaries, vendor payments, things that are necessary to continue the business operations. And don't forget, quarterly taxes. An additional part of this process is looking at what employees and vendors are actually essential for the business to operate, succeed and grow. Let's say you have 10 employees that have annual salaries of 50,000. That means you'll need to assume around 60,000 of cash out of the business each month to cover your payroll and related costs like healthcare, etc. associated with those employees. And not covering these expenses could cause employees to leave exiting the business, which means you'll be unable to operate diminishing the cash flow even more so because of lack of people to do the work. That's a quick recipe for disaster if you don't consider the business's short term cash flow requirements. Next up you'll need to look at any interest and debt reduction needs. This is any payment due on any loans or debt the business has taken on, which includes the financing requirements that you received to pay for the deal. This is where you'll factor in paying interest on any of the money you've received in addition to paying principal on the loan. And that number might vary based on how much money you were loaned, what your goals are to pay it back, and what your goals are for the business in the next 3 months, 6 months, 12 months, etc. It will also depend on the types of financing you receive. If you have seller financing as part of the deal that's paid out monthly, you'll need to factor that into the payments to the seller as well. Or if you've promised potential earnouts to the seller, you'll need to factor those earnouts in as well, especially when you hit your numbers. And you don't want to miss those payments because it could deeply impact the business and the cash flow. For example, if part of your financing comes from asset-backed loans, you could have those assets seized by a lender to cover the cost of the loan. So it's important that you know what your cash out will be to service the financing in your debt. Similarly, to paying back the loans from lenders, you'll need to be mindful of any financial covenants from the financiers. As we talked about the financial covenants lesson in the previous module, financiers will impose covenants on any money they loan you so that they can keep an eye on what's happening from the inside of the business. Covenants require you to meet certain thresholds or ratios in your business, and you must be mindful of when these covenants must be met. If a lender requires that you meet a certain level of cash flow each quarter, you need to work that into your assessment when making the forecast. If you look at the financials, do the cash flow forecast and find that you aren't able to meet those expectations and you might need to reassess or renegotiate the situation. The sooner you get in front of it, the better. So it's critically important to know the dates your covenants are assessed by your lender. And if your cash flow forecasting prompts any concern as to the viability of the covenants, you need to communicate this. The next consideration is determining your business's dependencies. This means you're thinking about where there might be potential cash balance shortfalls in advance. If you go through your cash flow forecasting and you see in months seven or eight of owning the business that you're going to run into low cash balance issues, you'll know that your growth goals need to overcome the cash imbalance you're seeing in the forecast. This must be attacked in the present moment with velocity. This might be the most important use of cash flow forecasting. You probably already have scale tactics and ideas in mind to overcome these issues. If you don't, you must put them in place before it's an issue. By doing this kind of forecasting before you buy the business, then after you buy the business, you can create operational leverage in the business to make sure you hit your forecast goals. If there is a problem, you'll be able to pinpoint exactly what's causing the problem and fix it now so you don't run into the issue in the future. Is it vendor costs, salaries? Is it just bloated expenses? Either way, you'll know what that looks like. You'll know what you need to do to address it before it's an issue. You can see what needs to change, how long you have to change it and what you need to do about it right now. Think of this as your early warning alarm system. Okay, so let's move on. We've kind of covered growth and scaling as we've talked about the other dependencies, but I want to go into more detail about why this is a key dependency. Earlier in this course, we worked through mapping out all the requirements for your deal, what your goals are, the sectors you're interested in, the types of businesses you may want, the type of owners you want to target. You've created goals and expectations about where maybe you could take a new business. By completing this cashflow forecasting, though, it gives you a chance to look at all the numbers and think about how much working capital you actually need to fund your goals. So it not only helps you determine the cashflow needed to fund your business and your financing, but it also helps you determine if you have enough cashflow to have a healthy, scalable, secure, valuable business. Those are five key considerations to think about before you start forecasting. It's important to be thinking about all of these as you look at the numbers. You can easily look at these numbers, see the cashflow and think, oh, all is good, let's move on. But if you're not looking at future cashflow, you could easily get in trouble. You must be thinking about how that money is going to get utilized and where you want the business to be. You can easily create a time sink and sometimes a money sink for yourself without actually realizing it. The further out you can see in the future, in your actual cashflow forecasting model, the better off and more secure you're going to be. As I mentioned earlier, there are three essential pieces to the model. Beginning cash balance each month, the business's sources of cash each month, and the business's use of cash each month. The beginning cash balance is the cash you expect to have on hand each month. This is anything where you've got cash being held in bank accounts, PayPal accounts, etc. that you have access to for your business. It's any surplus cash hanging around from the previous month. For example, if after all sources of cash and uses of cash net out to a positive amount, that's your beginning balance going into the next month. If you have 10,000 in cash in the bank in January, you gain 15,000 from cash sources and you use 20,000, take the beginning cash balance and cash sources minus cash uses. So you have $15,000 in February. The sources of cash are any cash flowing into the business each month. These are things like sales, receivables you're collecting, repayments from any money you loaned. If you receive $10,000 of cash from product sales and you have 18,000 of invoices being paid, then you have 28,000 of additional cash going in next month. That brings us to your monthly cash usage. This is any expense that your business may incur. You would deduct payroll every month and if you had any commission employees that are maybe paid out quarterly, you need to factor that in as well so you can track it for your forecasting. You might pay suppliers and you might pay them more during certain seasons or months, so you need to consider that as well. You pay rent and utilities every month and you need to add this. This would also include any loan payments that you need to make to financiers or sellers if you have an earn out or any money that the business needs to use for its growth. All of this gets deducted from whatever carried over cash balance you had plus your sources of cash each month. Then whatever number you have left becomes your carried over cash balance for the next month. As promised, we've put together a template for you to use for your own cash flow forecasting.
